problem.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problem.description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.

solutions.title: Interview Strategy Guide
solutions.description: For the Top K Frequent Elements problem, interviewers generally look for a candidate who can progress from a standard efficient solution to an optimized one. The "preferred" answer is actually a strategic two-step delivery: starting with a Heap (Priority Queue) approach and then pivoting to Bucket Sort for the optimal O(n) time complexity. Step 1: The Essential Foundation Regardless of the algorithm, you must first count the frequencies of the elements. This is always done using a Hash Map (or a Counter in Python). • Time Complexity: O(n) • Space Complexity: O(n) to store the unique elements. Method A: The "Standard" High-Level Answer (Min-Heap) Most interviewers expect this as your first implementation. It shows you understand how to use data structures to limit sorting overhead. Logic: Maintain a Min-Heap of size k. As you iterate through your frequency map, add elements to the heap. If the heap size exceeds k, pop the element with the smallest frequency. Why Min-Heap? A Max-Heap would require O(n log n) to extract k elements if you push everything. A Min-Heap of size k keeps the complexity at O(n log k). Complexity: • Time: O(n log k) — where n is the number of unique elements. • Space: O(n + k) — to store the map and the heap. Method B: The "Optimal" Answer (Bucket Sort) This is the "aha!" moment interviewers look for. Since the maximum frequency of any element is capped by the length of the input array (n), you can use Bucket Sort to achieve linear time. Logic: 1. Create an array of "buckets" where the index represents the frequency. 2. Place each element into the bucket corresponding to its frequency count. 3. Iterate through the buckets from right to left (highest frequency to lowest) and collect elements until you have k. Complexity: • Time: O(n) — you iterate through the array once to count, once to fill buckets, and once to collect the top k. • Space: O(n) — to store the buckets. Comparison of Approaches • Sorting → Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n) — Brute force; usually insufficient. • Min-Heap → Time: O(n log k), Space: O(n + k) — Strong. Shows solid data structure knowledge. • Bucket Sort → Time: O(n), Space: O(n) — Expert. Shows you identified the frequency constraint. • Quickselect → Time: O(n) avg, Space: O(n) — Advanced. Good to mention as a follow-up for general Top-K. How to Answer Like a Pro 1. State the Map First: "First, I'll use a hash map to get the frequency of each element in O(n) time." 2. Propose the Heap: "The standard way to find the top k would be a Min-Heap of size k. This gives us O(n log k)." 3. Offer the Optimization: "However, since our frequencies are bounded by the input size n, we can actually optimize this to O(n) using Bucket Sort. Would you like me to implement the linear time version?" This approach demonstrates that you know the "safe" standard tool (Heap) but are also capable of "problem-specific" optimizations (Bucket Sort).

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Min-Heap (Priority Queue)
solutions.description: Use a min heap of size k to find top k frequent elements. First count frequencies with a hash map, then maintain a min-heap that keeps only the k most frequent elements.
solutions.code_python:
import heapq
from collections import Counter

def topKFrequent(nums: list[int], k: int) -> list[int]:
    frequency = Counter(nums)

    # Min-heap of size k: (freq, num)
    heap = []
    for num, freq in frequency.items():
        heapq.heappush(heap, (freq, num))
        if len(heap) > k:
            heapq.heappop(heap)

    return [num for freq, num in heap]

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Bucket Sort (Optimal O(n))
solutions.description: Since the maximum frequency of any element is bounded by the array length n, we can use bucket sort where the index represents frequency. This avoids any sorting or heap operations, achieving true linear time.

example_walkthrough.text:
Input: nums = [1,1,1,2,2,3], k = 2

Step 1 - Frequency Map: {1: 3, 2: 2, 3: 1}

Step 2 - Buckets (index = frequency):
  bucket[0] = []
  bucket[1] = [3]
  bucket[2] = [2]
  bucket[3] = [1]
  bucket[4] = []
  bucket[5] = []
  bucket[6] = []

Step 3 - Iterate from right to left:
  bucket[6]: empty → skip
  bucket[5]: empty → skip
  bucket[4]: empty → skip
  bucket[3]: [1] → add 1, index=1
  bucket[2]: [2] → add 2, index=2 → we have k=2 elements, stop

Result: [1, 2]
